title: GPU Pipeline Architecture
use_for: structuring compute stages, buffer contracts, dirty flags, indirect dispatch, and indirect draw
turning GPU work into predictable staged runtime architecture
rules: define stage contracts before writing kernels
define buffer contracts before dispatch logic
keep CPU orchestration and GPU bulk execution separate
prefer GPU-produced counts and args over CPU readback in hot paths
make invalidation and rerun scope explicit
make diagnostics optional and asynchronous unless correctness requires blocking
procedure: Split work into GPU stages with explicit inputs and outputs.
Define buffers, strides, counts, owners, and resize policies.
Define stage dirty flags or state transitions.
Define dispatch and indirect dispatch arguments.

primary_when: the request is about a multi-stage GPU-driven pipeline
the request is about buffer contracts, dirty flags, indirect dispatch, or CPU/GPU synchronization boundaries
the request combines compute stages with indirect rendering or GPU-side visibility
must_not_own_when: the request is only about CPU-side pipeline architecture with no GPU buffer or dispatch concern
the request is only about UI or mod loader behavior

solves: how to design a GPU workflow as explicit stages rather than scattered dispatch calls
how to keep buffer ownership, stage dependencies, and invalidation reviewable
how to avoid hidden CPU/GPU synchronization and unnecessary full-pipeline reruns
does_not_solve: low-level graphics API portability by itself
CPU feature architecture outside GPU handoff boundaries

core_principles: A GPU pipeline is a dataflow graph with GPU buffers as products.
The CPU should orchestrate work; the GPU should own bulk parallel transforms.
Every kernel should have a documented input and output buffer contract.
Dirty flags and stage state should bound rerun cost.
Indirect dispatch and draw args should be generated close to the data that owns the count.
Diagnostics should expose pipeline health without forcing per-frame stalls.
constraints: Do not dispatch kernels without validating required buffers and kernels.
Do not read back GPU counters in hot paths when indirect args can keep the work on GPU.
Do not rebuild spawn or source buffers when only visibility or LOD changed.
Do not let shader struct layouts drift from CPU-side struct stride definitions.
Do not hide allocation and release ownership across unrelated systems.
Do not allow optional diagnostic buffers to become required for rendering correctness.
decision_rules: Use gpu-pipeline-architecture when a task has more than one GPU stage or crosses compute and rendering.
Attach gpu-compute for kernel dispatch, buffer layout, and GPU-side stage execution details.
Attach gpu-instancing when the pipeline ends in instanced or indirect rendering.
Attach data-oriented-pipeline support when the GPU stages mirror a broader staged data model.
Attach performance support when the main risk is dispatch cost, memory bandwidth, allocation churn, or readback stalls.
Introduce dirty flags when different changes require different stage rerun scopes.

preferred_patterns: stage contract table with named buffer products
structured source buffers, append visibility buffers, raw count buffers, and indirect args buffers
dirty flags or explicit state enum per pipeline unit
avoid_patterns: single broad rebuild path for all changes
CPU readback to decide every draw or dispatch count
buffer allocation hidden in multiple unrelated callers
kernel parameter binding scattered across feature code
render shaders assuming buffers exist without safe binding policy

recipes.name: Design a GPU stage pipeline
recipes.when: Use when a feature transforms source data through several GPU stages before rendering or readback.
steps: Name each stage and the data product it creates.
Define input and output buffers for each stage.
Define dirty flags that identify which stages must rerun after a change.
Define dispatch dimensions and when indirect dispatch args are generated.
Define the render or readback handoff.
Add diagnostics counters for stage throughput and rejected work.

recipes.name: Bound visibility-only refresh cost
recipes.when: Use when camera movement should update culling or LOD without respawning source data.
steps: Keep source and instance buffers alive across visibility updates.
Mark only cull or LOD dirty flags.
Reset append counters before visibility kernels.
Build draw args from visible counts on GPU.
Throttle refresh by movement, rotation, or time thresholds if camera motion is noisy.

recipes.name: Add non-blocking diagnostics
recipes.when: Use when debugging GPU stages without stalling gameplay.
steps: Create a small debug counter buffer owned by the pipeline unit.
Increment counters in kernels for spawned, valid, visible, or rejected items.
Request async readback at a bounded interval.
Track in-flight requests to avoid piling up readbacks.
Treat readback failure as diagnostic loss, not pipeline failure.

pitfalls: Re-running spawn and quantize when only camera visibility changed.
Using CPU-visible counts as the control path for every frame.
Forgetting to reset append counters before visibility stages.
Letting draw bounds clip valid indirect instances.
Allocating new buffers every update after counts have stabilized.
Debugging with blocking readback and mistaking the result for real runtime cost.
review_checklist: Are CPU and GPU responsibilities explicit?
Does each stage have named input and output buffers?
Are buffer strides, counts, and owners documented?
Can rerun scope be explained from dirty flags or stage state?
Are indirect args generated without hot-path CPU readback?
Are append counters reset before use?
Are diagnostics optional, bounded, and non-blocking?
Is buffer release handled for domain reload, teardown, or chunk unload?

references.title: GPU Pipeline Architecture
references.interpretation: Use this first for staged GPU pipeline design, CPU/GPU boundaries, and rerun policy.
references.why: It defines the domain's main architecture lens.

references.title: GPU Buffer Contracts
references.interpretation: Use this for buffer roles, ownership, stride, count, and lifecycle policy.
references.why: GPU pipelines fail when buffers are implicit or underspecified.

references.title: GPU Diagnostics
references.interpretation: Use when adding counters, async readback, health state, or failure policy.
references.why: It keeps observability from becoming a synchronization bottleneck.
